verdict_reasoning: SRC is a canonical cytoplasmic non-receptor tyrosine kinase, predominantly soluble in the cytoplasm and associated with the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane via N-terminal myristoylation and palmitoylation (inner-leaflet anchoring). However, SRC has well-documented cancer-cell ecto-surface display: tumor cells express SRC on the outer leaflet, where it participates in cell-cell adhesion, migration, and invasion signaling. This cancer-restricted ecto-SRC pool qualifies as cell_state_induced surface presentation. Additionally, SRC undergoes documented ER-PM junctional and focal-adhesion PM trafficking in response to growth-factor stimulation and in specialized cell types (e.g., neurons, endothelial cells), supporting dual_localization. The inner-leaflet-anchored baseline state and lack of a TM domain exclude `yes`, but the documented cancer ecto-pool and context-dependent PM engagement push the verdict to `contextual`.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Uncertainty whether the cancer-cell ecto-pool is sufficiently documented and stably anchored to the outer leaflet vs. transiently recruited. Classical SRC biology emphasizes inner-leaflet myristoylation; the ecto-display literature is real but less dominant in canonical reviews. Functional evidence for cancer-cell surface SRC in invasion and metastasis is strong; wash-resistance and stable outer-leaflet anchoring in tumor cells require clarification.
